title: Supreme Court Cases
description: Landmark Supreme Court decisions and their significance

cards.front: Marbury v. Madison (1803)
cards.back: Established judicial review — the power of the Supreme Court to declare laws unconstitutional

cards.front: Which landmark case established judicial review?
cards.back: Marbury v. Madison (1803)

cards.front: McCulloch v. Maryland (1819)
cards.back: Established that Congress has implied powers under the Necessary and Proper Clause; states cannot tax the federal government

cards.front: Which case ruled that states cannot tax the federal government and upheld implied powers of Congress?
cards.back: McCulloch v. Maryland (1819)

cards.front: Gibbons v. Ogden (1824)
cards.back: Established that Congress has broad power to regulate interstate commerce under the Commerce Clause

cards.front: Which case broadly defined Congress's power to regulate interstate commerce?
cards.back: Gibbons v. Ogden (1824)

cards.front: Worcester v. Georgia (1832)
cards.back: Ruled that states have no authority over Native American lands; only the federal government can deal with Native nations

cards.front: Which case ruled that states have no authority over Native American lands?
cards.back: Worcester v. Georgia (1832)

cards.front: Dred Scott v. Sandford (1857)
cards.back: Ruled that enslaved people were not citizens and had no right to sue; declared the Missouri Compromise unconstitutional

cards.front: Which case ruled that enslaved people were not citizens and could not sue in federal court?
cards.back: Dred Scott v. Sandford (1857)

cards.front: Plessy v. Ferguson (1896)
cards.back: Upheld racial segregation under the 'separate but equal' doctrine

cards.front: Which case established the 'separate but equal' doctrine allowing racial segregation?
cards.back: Plessy v. Ferguson (1896)

cards.front: Schenck v. United States (1919)
cards.back: Established the 'clear and present danger' test for limiting free speech; upheld the Espionage Act

cards.front: Which case established the 'clear and present danger' test for free speech?
cards.back: Schenck v. United States (1919)

cards.front: Korematsu v. United States (1944)
cards.back: Upheld the internment of Japanese Americans during World War II; widely regarded as a grave injustice

cards.front: Which case upheld the internment of Japanese Americans during World War II?
cards.back: Korematsu v. United States (1944)

cards.front: Brown v. Board of Education (1954)
cards.back: Declared racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional, overturning Plessy v. Ferguson's 'separate but equal' doctrine

cards.front: Which case declared racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional?
cards.back: Brown v. Board of Education (1954)

cards.front: Mapp v. Ohio (1961)
cards.back: Applied the exclusionary rule to states — illegally obtained evidence cannot be used in state courts (4th Amendment)

cards.front: Which case ruled that illegally obtained evidence cannot be used in state courts?
cards.back: Mapp v. Ohio (1961)

cards.front: Engel v. Vitale (1962)
cards.back: Ruled that government-directed prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause of the 1st Amendment

cards.front: Which case banned government-directed prayer in public schools?
cards.back: Engel v. Vitale (1962)

cards.front: Gideon v. Wainwright (1963)
cards.back: Ruled that states must provide attorneys to criminal defendants who cannot afford one (6th Amendment right to counsel)

cards.front: Which case established the right to a court-appointed attorney for those who cannot afford one?
cards.back: Gideon v. Wainwright (1963)

cards.front: Miranda v. Arizona (1966)
cards.back: Required police to inform suspects of their rights (right to remain silent, right to an attorney) before interrogation

cards.front: Which case required police to read suspects their rights before interrogation?
cards.back: Miranda v. Arizona (1966)

cards.front: Tinker v. Des Moines (1969)
cards.back: Ruled that students do not lose their 1st Amendment rights at school; protected student symbolic speech (wearing armbands)

cards.front: Which case ruled that students retain free speech rights in school, including symbolic speech?
cards.back: Tinker v. Des Moines (1969)

cards.front: New York Times Co. v. Sullivan (1964)
cards.back: Set the 'actual malice' standard for libel suits by public officials, strengthening freedom of the press

cards.front: Which case established the 'actual malice' standard for defamation of public officials?
cards.back: New York Times Co. v. Sullivan (1964)

cards.front: New York Times Co. v. United States (1971)
cards.back: Ruled against prior restraint, allowing the New York Times to publish the Pentagon Papers (1st Amendment)

cards.front: Which case allowed publication of the Pentagon Papers, ruling against prior restraint?
cards.back: New York Times Co. v. United States (1971)

cards.front: Roe v. Wade (1973)
cards.back: Ruled that the Constitution protects a right to abortion under the right to privacy; later overturned by Dobbs v. Jackson (2022)

cards.front: Which case ruled that the Constitution protects a right to abortion, later overturned in 2022?
cards.back: Roe v. Wade (1973)

cards.front: United States v. Nixon (1974)
cards.back: Ruled that executive privilege is not absolute; the president must comply with subpoenas for evidence in criminal trials

cards.front: Which case ruled that executive privilege is not absolute and required the president to turn over White House tapes?
cards.back: United States v. Nixon (1974)

cards.front: Regents of the University of California v. Bakke (1978)
cards.back: Ruled that racial quotas in college admissions are unconstitutional, but race can be one factor in admissions decisions

cards.front: Which case ruled that racial quotas in college admissions are unconstitutional but race may be considered as one factor?
cards.back: Regents of the University of California v. Bakke (1978)

cards.front: New Jersey v. T.L.O. (1985)
cards.back: Ruled that school officials can search students with 'reasonable suspicion' rather than 'probable cause' (4th Amendment)

cards.front: Which case established that school officials need only 'reasonable suspicion' to search students?
cards.back: New Jersey v. T.L.O. (1985)

cards.front: Texas v. Johnson (1989)
cards.back: Ruled that flag burning is protected symbolic speech under the 1st Amendment

cards.front: Which case ruled that flag burning is protected speech under the 1st Amendment?
cards.back: Texas v. Johnson (1989)

cards.front: Citizens United v. FEC (2010)
cards.back: Ruled that political spending by corporations and unions is protected free speech; struck down limits on independent campaign expenditures

cards.front: Which case ruled that corporate political spending is protected free speech?
cards.back: Citizens United v. FEC (2010)

cards.front: Obergefell v. Hodges (2015)
cards.back: Ruled that same-sex marriage is a constitutional right under the 14th Amendment's Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses

cards.front: Which case established same-sex marriage as a constitutional right nationwide?
cards.back: Obergefell v. Hodges (2015)

cards.front: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (2022)
cards.back: Overturned Roe v. Wade, ruling that the Constitution does not confer a right to abortion; returned the issue to the states

cards.front: Which 2022 case overturned Roe v. Wade?
cards.back: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization

cards.front: What amendment is central to Mapp v. Ohio (search and seizure)?
cards.back: 4th Amendment

cards.front: What amendment is central to Gideon v. Wainwright (right to counsel)?
cards.back: 6th Amendment

cards.front: What amendment is central to Miranda v. Arizona (rights during interrogation)?
cards.back: 5th Amendment (self-incrimination) and 6th Amendment (right to counsel)

cards.front: What amendment is central to Engel v. Vitale (school prayer)?
cards.back: 1st Amendment (Establishment Clause)

cards.front: What amendment is central to Tinker v. Des Moines (student speech)?
cards.back: 1st Amendment (Free Speech)

cards.front: What case overturned Plessy v. Ferguson?
cards.back: Brown v. Board of Education (1954)
